text: Socrata-style open data APIs expose tabular JSON at https://HOST/resource/RESOURCE_ID.json with optional $limit. Hosts often look like data.city.gov or opendata.county.gov — the hostname does not need to contain the word Socrata. Search queries should include the city or county name plus "open data" and the record type.

ArcGIS Hub dataset pages use URLs like hub.arcgis.com/datasets/ followed by a 32-character hexadecimal item id, sometimes with _0 for layer index. These ids map to ArcGIS Online items whose JSON includes a FeatureServer service URL for /query. Catalog landing pages without that UUID in the URL are not directly queryable — you need the dataset or FeatureServer link.

ArcGIS FeatureServer layer URLs contain .../FeatureServer/N where N is the layer index. MapServer endpoints may list layers; prefer URLs that already end in FeatureServer/N for row samples. Government GIS often lives under .../arcgis/rest/services/...

Many government portals return HTML catalog or "about" pages from Google, not the API URL. Inner links on those pages often point to Hub datasets, Socrata resource pages, or embedded FeatureServer URLs. Search strategies should prefer queries that surface API or dataset pages: site:gov FeatureServer, site:*.gov "open data" permit, or the agency name plus "GIS REST".

URLs like dev.socrata.com/foundry/DATA_DOMAIN/RESOURCE_ID are documentation pages, not the API. The JSON API is always https://DATA_DOMAIN/resource/RESOURCE_ID.json — use the DATA_DOMAIN host from the Foundry path, not dev.socrata.com.

Building permit and construction open datasets rarely include owner or contractor email. Contacts usually require enrichment or separate business directories. Do not promise email fields unless the user explicitly accepts enrichment or the source is a business registry.

When the user names a US state, include the state abbreviation and both state-level and city/county phrases in keywords_for_search. Metro areas often span multiple counties — "metro", "MSA", or "greater [city]" can improve search recall.

Permit datasets may mix residential and commercial. Users asking for commercial focus need filters on use type, valuation, or project class when those fields exist; mention likely field names like work class, occupancy, or valuation in strict matching guidance.

Google search snippets for open data often point to marketing pages. Prefer suggested queries that include technical terms likely to surface real endpoints: FeatureServer, /resource/, hub.arcgis.com/datasets, filetype:json, or "geojson".

Some ArcGIS layers require tokens, referer checks, or are behind VPN. If a layer returns errors about authentication or permissions, the model should suggest trying a different jurisdiction or a Socrata portal for the same geography.
Permit data may lag days to weeks. Trigger events for "new" permits should reference filing or issue dates in field_schema, not assume real-time feeds unless the source is advertised as live.
B2B lead gen from permits targets contractors, owners, or developers. Field schemas might include contractor name, owner name, valuation, and address — not personal emails from the permit row itself.

Discovery strategies should diversify source types: government open data, industry news, hiring boards, and local business journals when the trigger events fit. Avoid suggesting only one type unless the user narrowed the niche.

Some agencies embed structured data or JSON endpoints in script tags; direct API paths beat scraping. When suggesting URLs, favor known data portals and API documentation pages over generic homepages.

US states sometimes aggregate local data in state GIS or transparency portals. Queries can combine state name with "GIS services", "statewide permits", or "geospatial open data" when city-level search underperforms.

North Carolina cities often publish permits via Socrata (data.city.gov style) or regional ArcGIS hubs. If one city's portal fails, neighboring municipalities in the same metro may use parallel datasets with similar fields.
Search queries must not include private personal data or instructions to bypass terms of service. Focus on public records and openly advertised government datasets.

Honolulu / Oahu building permits and planning: prioritize Honolulu Department of Planning and Permitting (DPP) open data, county GIS services, and site:honolulu.gov permit or zoning related paths. Combine "Honolulu" with "building permit", "open data", or "ArcGIS" for API-grade URLs.

Maui County Hawaii: building permits and planning often appear on county GIS or ArcGIS Hub; queries should include "Maui County" plus permit, building, or "open data" and site:mauicounty.gov or FeatureServer.

Hawaii large-project signals (for B2B suppliers): Pacific Business News, developer master plans (e.g. Ward Village, Kakaako), hospitality and high-rise construction news — use as supplementary search angles combined with permit or GC names, not as a substitute for government row data.
